title: Certifying coherence in quantum devices under classical control
authors: Gabriele Cobucci, Nicola D'Alessandro, Raphael Brinster, Alexander Bernal, Nikolai Wyderka, Armin Tavakoli
abstract: Quantum states that do not commute exhibit coherence, but only when the device preparing them is assumed to be unaffected by classical parameters inaccessible to the experimenter. Such hidden classical control arises both in fundamental tests of quantum phenomena and in quantum information protocols that operate under limited control assumptions. Here, we address the problem of coherence certification by developing complete and practically efficient methods. First, we prove that coherence can be fully characterised through a hierarchy of semidefinite programs. Second, we introduce a practical semidefinite programming approach that achieves useful accuracy while remaining computationally efficient even for preparation devices generating many, potentially high-dimensional, quantum states. For the important special case of qubits, we further exploit conceptual connections with the theory of joint measurability to obtain highly accurate coherence characterisation that scales to more than one thousand qubits. Finally, we apply these methods to determine whether quantum channels are able to preserve coherence or are inherently coherence-breaking. Together, these results provide a powerful toolbox for analysing quantum superposition in the presence of hidden classical control.
summary: This work provides a powerful mathematical toolbox to certify quantum coherence when the preparation process is corrupted by unknown classical variables. It achieves this by developing and comparing SDP-based criteria, offering both theoretically complete and computationally efficient methods. The results are crucial for analyzing the robustness of quantum superposition in real-world quantum devices and channels.
main_problem: The paper develops complete and practical methods to certify quantum coherence in devices whose preparation process is subject to hidden classical control parameters.
main_result: It establishes that coherence can be fully characterized by a hierarchy of Semidefinite Programs (SDPs) and provides computationally efficient SDP criteria for practical use.
method: The core methodology involves formulating coherence certification as checking the feasibility of increasingly complex SDP constraints, often utilizing dual formulations and iterative optimization.
model_or_system: The study analyzes quantum states ($ho_x$) generated by a preparation device under hidden classical control ($\lambda$), and the effect of quantum channels ($\Lambda$) on these states.
key_observables: Coherence quantifiers like $v^*(E)$ (based on isotropic noise) and the coherence witness $W(\Lambda(E))$ are central diagnostics.
important_parameters: The dimensionality of the Hilbert space ($d$), the number of states ($N$), and the noise parameters ($v, u, \gamma$) are key parameters.
main_assumptions: The practical SDP criterion is an approximation of the full, infinite SDP hierarchy, and some analyses rely on approximating the Bloch sphere with a polyhedron.
figures_summary: Figures compare the bounds on critical isotropic noise ($v$) derived from different SDP methods, showing superior accuracy for the proposed practical method. Another figure plots coherence bounds for composed channels.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the problem (coherence under classical control) to developing the full SDP hierarchy, proposing a practical SDP criterion, and finally applying these tools to analyze coherence preservation through various quantum channels.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical tools developed for characterizing coherence in the presence of unknown classical noise are directly applicable to benchmarking quantum hardware and understanding decoherence mechanisms in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Classical model simulating the non-commuting states ρ1 = v |0⟩⟨0| + 1−v

FIG. 2: Bounds on the critical isotropic noise required to certify coherence for the two sets Ebases and EETF. The points were obtained using the methods introduced in section III A (triangles) and in section III B (squares). The results from the second method are not monotone for EETF because different families are used to construct the equiangular tight frame for different d.

FIG. 3: Bounds on coherence properties in the channels Λdepol v ◦Λdepha u (red) and Λdepol v ◦Λamp γ (blue). We have computed bounds both for the channels being coherence-preserving and coherence-breaking, but only one is displayed since they are nearly identical (due to the polyhedron’s shrinking factor being r ≈0.9879). For any given depolarising parameter v, channels with dephasing parameter u below the red curve are coherence breaking, whereas those with values of the dampening parameter γ below the blue line are coherence preserving.
